name: Disaster Recovery & Business Continuity

useCases.use: All Tier 1 production workloads (critical business systems with RTO < 4 hours and RPO < 1 hour)
useCases.dontUse: Development, test, or staging environments — DR is not required for non-production workloads

useCases.use: Data platform workloads requiring active-active or active-passive replication across Azure region pairs
useCases.dontUse: Ephemeral compute workloads with no persistent state that can be redeployed from IaC within minutes

useCases.use: Regulatory workloads requiring documented and tested DR procedures for audit evidence (SOX, ISO 27001)
useCases.dontUse: Internal tools with RTO > 72 hours where backup-and-restore is a sufficient recovery strategy

approvers: Platform Architecture Board, CISO, James Okafor
components: Azure Site Recovery (ASR) — VM and on-premises server replication and automated failover orchestration
Azure Backup — policy-based backup for VMs, SQL databases, Blob Storage, and AKS persistent volumes
Azure Traffic Manager / Front Door — DNS-level or anycast traffic failover between primary and secondary regions
Geo-redundant storage (GRS / RA-GRS) — automatic data replication across Azure region pairs
Azure SQL Geo-Replication / Active Geo-Replication — synchronous and asynchronous database replication
Azure Cosmos DB multi-region writes — active-active global database replication
Azure Automation + Azure DevOps — DR runbook automation and IaC-based environment rebuild

guardrails.rule: All Tier 1 workloads must document and test RTO ≤ 4 hours and RPO ≤ 1 hour annually
DR failover test must be executed at least annually; results must be documented and exceptions remediated within 90 days
All Tier 1 data stores must have backup immutability enabled and backup compliance monitored daily
All Azure workloads must use Microsoft-defined region pairs for geo-replication — custom region combinations are not approved
All Tier 1 workloads must have a tested DR runbook registered in the CMDB and updated within 30 days of architecture changes

revisionHistory.author: Alex Thompson
revisionHistory.summary: Initial DR/BCP pattern for Azure Tier 1 workloads

revisionHistory.author: Alex Thompson
revisionHistory.summary: Endorsed. Added Cosmos DB multi-region writes, backup immutability guardrail, and annual DR test requirement.

strategicIntent: Define a standardised Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity architecture for all Tier 1 and Tier 2 Azure workloads — ensuring that McCain's critical systems can recover from regional Azure outages, data corruption, or catastrophic failure within defined RTO and RPO targets, with tested and automated failover procedures.
exceptionProcess: Submit DR Exception Request via ServiceNow for any Tier 1 workload unable to meet RTO/RPO targets. Requires Platform Architecture Board approval, formal risk acceptance by business owner, and compensating controls documented. Maximum exception duration 6 months.
interactionSteps: Workload team defines Tier (1/2/3) and target RTO/RPO via the DR Tier Classification template registered in the CMDB
Platform team provisions geo-redundant infrastructure in the designated Azure region pair (Canada Central ↔ Canada East for Canadian workloads)
ASR or database geo-replication continuously replicates data from primary to secondary region; replication lag monitored in Azure Monitor
Azure Backup runs policy-based backup jobs; backup compliance report generated daily and reviewed by platform team
In a DR event: Traffic Manager or Front Door DNS failover redirects traffic to secondary region within minutes
ASR orchestrates VM failover to secondary region; database failover group promotes secondary to primary automatically or on-demand
DR test executed at minimum annually: automated failover triggered in isolated test environment; RTO/RPO validated against targets
Post-failover: Azure Automation runbooks validate service health in secondary before marking failover complete
problemStatement: Workload teams design recovery strategies in isolation, resulting in inconsistent RTO/RPO targets, untested DR procedures, manual failover requiring specialist knowledge unavailable during an incident, and no standard for which Azure region pairs to use for replication — creating organisational risk in the event of a regional Azure outage.
securityConsiderations: DR replica environments must inherit the same security policies, Azure Policy initiatives, and network controls as production
Backup data must be encrypted with customer-managed keys stored in Azure Key Vault; backup immutability must be enabled
DR runbook access must be gated by PIM — no standing access to DR orchestration tooling outside of declared incidents
Failover credentials and connection strings must be managed in Key Vault — not embedded in runbooks or pipelines
DR site must be treated as production security tier from day one — not as a relaxed secondary environment
